---
title: "Final R Markdown"
author: "Jozlyn Sanchez"
data: "5/13/26"
output:   
  html_document:
    theme: yeti
    code_folding: hide
editor_options: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---

# [**Marine Tagging in Clearwater, Florida**]{.underline}

During 2025, I was able to embark on an amazing internship opportunity
with Moore Marine College. During this experience, I was able to gain
knowledge about the local communities of sharks and rays in the
Clearwater, Florida area. Through marine anatomy courses, deep water
boating experiences, and hands on marine life tagging, I was able to
contribute to Moore Marine College's mission of continuous marine life
tracking.

## **Local Marine Life**

Local communities of sharks and rays were our focus during this mission.
Below I have listed all common species found in the Clearwater area as
well as how many were tagged during the duration of my internship.

[Sharks]{.underline}

-   Bull Shark - 0
-   Sandbar Shark - 2
-   Nurse Shark - 5
-   Blacktip Shark - 8
-   Blacknose Shark - 11
-   Great Hammerhead - 4

[Rays]{.underline}

-   Cownose Ray - 4

-   Spotted Eagle Ray - 3

-   Southern Ray - 11

-   Butterfly Ray - 0

![Southern Ray](images/IMG_7499-01.jpeg){width="539"}

```{r}
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

wqp_data <- data.frame(
  type = c(rep("Shark", 6), rep("Ray", 4)),
  species = c("Bull Shark", "Sandbar Shark", "Nurse Shark", 
              "Blacktip Shark", "Blacknose Shark", "Great Hammerhead",
              "Cownose Ray", "Spotted Eagle Ray", "Southern Ray", "Butterfly Ray"),
  count = c(0, 2, 5, 8, 11, 4, 4, 3, 11, 0)
)

print(wqp_data)

ggplot(wqp_data, aes(x = reorder(species, count), y = count, fill = type)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", width = 0.7) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.1))) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Shark" = "skyblue", "Ray" = "grey")) +
  labs(
    title = "Marine Species Tagged During My 2025 Internship",
    subtitle = "Reference: Local Marine Life",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Total Individuals Tagged",
    fill = "Species"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "gray3"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )
```

## **Climate Change and Migration Abnormalities**

Changes in climate, even to the lowest degree, are having a significant
impact on marine life migration patterns. Since sharks and rays are
cold-blooded, water temperature plays a key role in what species can do.
When water temperature changes outside of abnormal zones, species cannot
travel the distance they were once able to, must change their resting
sea level location, and untimely migration. This data is also important
to understand in relation to the Great Hammerhead population, whose body
is extremely sensitive to any changes in temperature and water pressure.

![Great Hammerhead
Shark](images/20250613_003935_9164F4 2.jpeg){width="541"}

![Blacktip Shark](images/20250613_003233_9226C2.jpeg){width="488"}

## **Informative Migration Video**

[How Does Climate Change Cause Marine Species To Migrate? - Earth
Science Answers Video](https://youtu.be/5KxK4JR8JyE?si=PjdLRQajdD8x1vbI)

```{r}
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

wqp_data <- data.frame(
  year = seq(1955, 2023, by = 4),
  ohc_change = c(-20, -15, -10, -5, 2, 10, 25, 45, 70, 105, 150, 210, 280, 360, 420, 480, 550, 620),
  uncertainty = c(15, 14, 12, 11, 10, 9, 8, 8, 7, 7, 6, 6, 5, 5, 5, 5, 4, 4)
)

ggplot(wqp_data, aes(x = year, y = ohc_change)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = ohc_change - uncertainty, ymax = ohc_change + uncertainty), 
              fill = "lightgrey", alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_line(color = "skyblue4", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(color = "skyblue4", size = 2) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = function(x) paste0(x, " ZJ")) +
  labs(
    title = "Annual Global Ocean Heat Content (0-2000m)",
    subtitle = "Units in Zettajoules (ZJ)",
    caption = "Data source: NOAA Climate.gov / NCEI",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Heat Content"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    axis.title = element_text(face = "bold")
  )

```

Furthermore, these migration patterns changes are not only being noticed
in Florida, but the rest of the world. According to NOAA FISHERIES'
article *Sharks, Rays, and Climate Change: Impacts on Habitat, Prey
Distribution, and Health,* these changes are being notices across the
world with the Scalloped Hammerhead Shark population.

```{r}

wqp_data <- data.frame(
  dps = c("Eastern Pacific", "Eastern Atlantic", "Central & SW Atlantic", 
          "Indo-West Pacific", "NW Atlantic & Gulf of Mexico", "Central Pacific"),
  status = c("Endangered", "Endangered", "Threatened", 
             "Threatened", "Not Listed (MSA Managed)", "Not Listed (MSA Managed)"),
  level = c(2, 2, 1, 1, 0, 0)
)

ggplot(wqp_data, aes(x = reorder(dps, level), y = level, fill = status)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", width = 0.6) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Endangered" = "orange", 
    "Threatened" = "red3", 
    "Not Listed (MSA Managed)" = "grey"
  )) +
  labs(
    title = "Conservation Status of Scalloped Hammerhead DPS",
    subtitle = "Data source: NOAA Fisheries (Endangered Species Act Listings)",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Protection Priority",
    fill = "ESA Status"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    axis.text.x = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )
```

![Nurse Shark](images/Image.jpeg){width="554"}

The NOAA also describes discrepancies found within the local populations
of bull sharks due to warming waters. This data works to show how deep
climate issues run and how no species is immune to our changing climate.

```{r}
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)

wqp_data <- data.frame(
  region = c("Southern FL", "Central FL", "Northern FL/GA", "Carolinas"),
  early_period = c(0.85, 0.45, 0.12, 0.02),
  recent_period = c(0.88, 0.62, 0.35, 0.15)
)

wqp_long <- pivot_longer(
  wqp_data, 
  cols = ends_with("period"), 
  names_to = "timeframe", 
  values_to = "suitability"
)

wqp_long$region <- factor(wqp_long$region, levels = c("Carolinas", "Northern FL/GA", "Central FL", "Southern FL"))

ggplot(wqp_long, aes(x = timeframe, y = region, fill = suitability)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white", lwd = 1.5) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "skyblue", high = "blue4") +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = c("early_period" = "2003-2005", "recent_period" = "2018-2020")) +
  labs(
    title = "Northward Expansion of Suitable Shark Habitat",
    subtitle = "Based on Habitat Suitability Index (HSI) shifts",
    x = "Observation Period",
    y = "Geographic Region",
    fill = "Suitability Index"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text.y = element_text(face = "italic"),
    panel.grid = element_blank()
  )

```

## **Conclusion**

In accordance with the data sets listed above, it is clear to see how
impactful climate change is in the decline of nature migration patterns
for sharks and rays. This is why research efforts such as the mission of
the Moore Marine College are so beneficial to the continued research of
climate effects on marine life.

![Nurse Shark](images/IMG_6992-01.jpeg){width="517"}

## **Sources**

<https://www.fisheries.noaa.gov/feature-story/sharks-rays-and-climate-change-impacts-habitat-prey-distribution-and-health>

<https://www.climate.gov/news-features/understanding-climate/climate-change-ocean-heat-content>

<https://www.climatechange2013.org/images/report/WG1AR5_Chapter03_FINAL.pdf>

<https://www.fisheries.noaa.gov/action/final-rule-list-giant-manta-ray-threatened-under-endangered-species-act>

<https://www.fisheries.noaa.gov/action/listing-scalloped-hammerhead-shark-under-esa>

<https://myfwc.com/research/saltwater/>

<https://myfwc.com/research/saltwater/sharks-rays/>
